learning_objectives: Identify and apply classical character archetypes (Hero, Mentor, Shadow, Trickster) to game character design
Distinguish between archetypes (universal patterns) and tropes (genre-specific conventions) and know when to use each
Analyze how game characters subvert or embrace archetypes to create memorable identities
Map the hero's journey structure to game character progression and player identification
readings: The Art of Game Design: A Book of Lenses Ch. 19-20 (Jesse Schell)
Character Design from the Ground Up Ch. 1-3 (Kevin Crossley)
The Writer's Journey Ch. 1-4 (Christopher Vogler)

lecture_notes.introduction: Characters are the emotional anchor of every game. A player may forget a level's layout or a boss's attack pattern, but they remember characters who made them laugh, cry, or seethe with righteous anger. Character design in games is unique because the player inhabits or interacts with these characters for tens or hundreds of hours, far longer than any film character. This extended relationship demands depth: a character who is compelling in a 2-hour movie may feel one-dimensional after 40 hours of gameplay. Today we study the foundational patterns that have driven character creation across cultures and millennia. The 12 Jungian archetypes (Hero, Mentor, Shadow, Trickster, Innocent, Explorer, Sage, Rebel, Lover, Caregiver, Ruler, Creator) are not rigid templates but gravitational centers that characters orbit. The best game characters draw from multiple archetypes: Kratos is both Hero and Shadow, Ellie is both Innocent and Rebel, GLaDOS is both Sage and Trickster. Understanding these patterns gives you a vocabulary for designing characters whose behavior feels psychologically real even in fantastical settings.
key_concepts: The 12 Jungian archetypes mapped to game characters: Hero (Link), Mentor (Cortana), Shadow (Sephiroth), Trickster (GLaDOS), Innocent (Yoshi), Explorer (Lara Croft), Sage (Deckard Cain), Rebel (Solid Snake), Lover (Yennefer), Caregiver (Elizabeth), Ruler (Ganondorf), Creator (The Architect)

Archetype vs trope: archetypes are universal psychological patterns that transcend culture. Tropes are genre-specific conventions (the grizzled space marine, the plucky sidekick). Archetypes provide depth; tropes provide familiarity. The best characters use both.
Archetype blending: memorable characters combine 2-3 archetypes. A Mentor who is also a Trickster (like Flemeth in Dragon Age) creates tension because the player cannot fully trust their guidance.

The hero's journey in games: the player-character follows Campbell's monomyth (ordinary world, call to adventure, threshold, trials, ordeal, reward, return), but game mechanics replace narrative events as the vehicle for each stage.

Protagonist design for player identification: the player-character must be a vessel for the player's agency. Too much defined personality (they resist the player's choices) alienates; too little (they have no voice) makes them forgettable.

Antagonist design: the antagonist defines the conflict. A compelling antagonist has their own motivation (they believe they are right), a personal connection to the protagonist, and enough power to seem unstoppable without actually being so.

Supporting cast roles: companions (emotional support, gameplay aid), rivals (competitive motivation), merchants (resource management), quest-givers (narrative progression). Each role serves a gameplay function that the character design must support.

Cultural awareness in archetypes: some archetype associations vary across cultures. The Trickster is celebrated in West African folklore but mistrusted in Western narratives. Character designers must consider their global audience.

Anti-heroes and moral ambiguity: characters who do not fit cleanly into hero or villain categories create more nuanced player engagement. Arthur Morgan (RDR2) is a criminal the player roots for because his humanity is visible.

Character as game mechanic: a character's personality should be expressed through their game mechanics, not just their dialogue. An aggressive character should have aggressive abilities. A cautious character should have defensive ones.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping the archetypes of 10 iconic game characters onto a diagram, identifying which archetypes each embodies and how the blend creates their distinctive identity. Then brainstorming three original characters using archetype blending: combining Hero+Trickster, Mentor+Shadow, and Innocent+Rebel to generate character concepts.
setup_requirements: Digital drawing tablet or pen and paper for sketching exercises
Archetype reference chart (provided in course materials)
Character concept brainstorming template

review.value: Combining 2-3 archetypes in one character creates depth and internal tension. A Mentor who is also a Shadow (like Kreia in KOTOR 2) challenges the player's trust. A Hero who is also a Trickster (like Nathan Drake) creates charm and unpredictability.

The player-character must balance defined personality with player agency. Too defined (the character refuses the player's choices) alienates. Too blank (the character has no reactions) feels hollow. The sweet spot varies by genre: RPGs lean blank, action games lean defined.

A compelling antagonist believes they are the hero of their own story. Thanos believes he is saving the universe. Andrew Ryan believes he is liberating humanity. This self-justified motivation makes them more threatening than a villain who is evil for evil's sake.

A character's personality should manifest in their gameplay abilities. Aggressive characters have offense-heavy kits. Cautious characters have defensive abilities. Social characters have dialogue options. If the mechanics contradict the personality, the character feels incoherent.

learning_objectives: Apply shape language, color psychology, and proportion exaggeration to communicate character personality visually
Perform silhouette tests to evaluate character readability at various scales
Design characters that are instantly distinguishable from each other in a shared roster
Use cultural and historical visual references intentionally and respectfully
readings: Character Design from the Ground Up Ch. 4-6 (Kevin Crossley)
The Skillful Huntsman (Scott Robertson) Ch. 2
GDC 2016: 'The Art Direction of Overwatch' (Arnold Tsang)

lecture_notes.introduction: A player forms an opinion about a character in under two seconds -- before any dialogue, before any backstory. That first impression comes from visual design: silhouette, shape language, color, and proportion. These visual elements communicate character traits faster than words. Circles and curves say 'friendly and approachable' (Kirby, Baymax). Triangles and sharp angles say 'dangerous and aggressive' (Ganondorf, Reaper). Squares and rectangles say 'strong and reliable' (Minecraft Steve, Heavy from TF2). Color works similarly: warm palettes suggest warmth and heroism; cool palettes suggest mystery or villainy; high saturation suggests power; desaturation suggests weariness. The most effective character designs layer all of these visual languages so that every element reinforces the same personality message. When shape language says 'gentle' but color says 'dangerous,' the player receives mixed signals and the character becomes harder to read. Today we practice the full visual design toolkit through analysis of published characters and original sketching exercises.
key_concepts: Shape language: circles convey friendliness and approachability (Kirby, Baymax). Triangles convey danger, aggression, and cunning (Ganondorf, Scar from Lion King). Squares convey stability, strength, and reliability (Minecraft Steve, Hellboy).

Silhouette test: fill the character's outline with solid black. If a viewer can identify the character, their weapon, and their general personality from the silhouette alone, the design has strong readability. Characters that fail this test are visually confused in gameplay.

Color psychology in character design: warm palettes (red, orange, yellow) suggest passion, heroism, or aggression. Cool palettes (blue, purple, green) suggest calm, mystery, or villainy. High saturation signals power; low saturation signals fatigue or humility.

Proportion exaggeration: how stylized proportions amplify personality traits. Large heads and small bodies convey youth and vulnerability. Long limbs and small heads convey elegance or eeriness. Exaggerated hands convey strength or dexterity.

Roster readability: in a game with multiple playable characters (fighting games, hero shooters), each character must be instantly distinguishable at a glance. Overwatch achieves this through unique silhouettes, color assignments, and size variation.

Faction design: characters belonging to the same faction share visual elements (color palette, insignia, material type) while maintaining individual identity. This creates visual cohesion without uniformity.

Cultural and historical reference: drawing from real-world cultures for visual inspiration requires research, respect, and consultation. Reducing a culture to a costume is harmful; integrating cultural elements with understanding is enriching.

Visual storytelling through costume: a character's clothing tells their story. Worn armor suggests experience. Mismatched clothes suggest improvisation. Pristine uniforms suggest authority. These details communicate backstory without exposition.

Readability at scale: game characters must be readable at multiple scales -- full-screen character select, medium-distance gameplay, and distant minimap icon. Design choices that work at one scale may fail at another.

Consistency across media: a character's visual design must work across concept art, 3D model, in-game rendering, marketing materials, and merchandise. The design must survive translation between media without losing its identity.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Silhouette analysis of 20 iconic game characters: identifying which are readable at thumbnail scale and extracting the shape language that makes them work. Then a sketching exercise: designing three characters for the same game (hero, villain, companion) with contrasting shape language, color palettes, and proportions that make them instantly distinguishable.
setup_requirements: Digital drawing tablet or pen and paper for sketching exercises
Adobe Photoshop, Clip Studio Paint, or Procreate for silhouette exercises
Reference image folder of analyzed characters (provided in course materials)

review.value: The psychological associations of basic geometric shapes applied to character design. Circles and curves: approachable, soft, friendly. Triangles and sharp angles: dangerous, aggressive, cunning. Squares and rectangles: strong, reliable, grounded. Every body part, accessory, and costume element communicates through shape.

Fill the character outline with solid black. If a viewer can identify the character, their weapon, and their role from the silhouette alone, the design has strong readability. Characters that fail this test blend together in gameplay, especially at distance or in busy scenes.

In multi-character games, every character must be instantly distinguishable. Overwatch achieves this through varied body types (tiny Tracer vs massive Reinhardt), unique color assignments, and distinctive silhouettes. No two characters share the same shape language.

Stylized proportions amplify personality. Big head + small body = cute and youthful (chibi characters). Long limbs + small head = elegant or unsettling (Slender Man). Wide shoulders + narrow waist = heroic strength (superhero proportion). Exaggeration makes characters more memorable than realistic proportions.

learning_objectives: Apply advanced shape language to design characters where primary, secondary, and tertiary shapes reinforce personality
Create character model sheets with turnaround views on a proportion grid for 3D production
Understand how silhouette complexity affects gameplay readability and production cost
Design characters with distinctive weapon and accessory silhouettes for gameplay identification
readings: Character Design from the Ground Up Ch. 5-6 (Kevin Crossley)
The Skillful Huntsman (Scott Robertson) Ch. 3
GDC 2019: 'Character Design Pipeline at Riot Games' (Gem Lim)

lecture_notes.introduction: Shape language operates on three levels. Primary shapes define the overall body silhouette: is the character round, triangular, or rectangular? Secondary shapes define major body sections: broad shoulders, narrow waist, large gauntlets. Tertiary shapes define surface detail: belt buckles, earrings, armor rivets. Effective character design establishes a consistent shape hierarchy where all three levels reinforce the same personality. A friendly character should have round primary, secondary, and tertiary shapes. Mixing round primary with spiky tertiary creates visual confusion -- is this character safe or dangerous? Today we dive deep into shape hierarchy and its relationship to production pipelines. A character with too much tertiary detail is expensive to model and animate, and the detail is invisible at gameplay distance. A character with no tertiary detail looks flat and unfinished in close-ups. The model sheet bridges concept art and 3D production by defining exactly how much detail exists at each level, from every angle, at production scale.
key_concepts: Primary shapes: the overall body silhouette. A character built on circles (round body, round head) reads as friendly from across a room. A character built on triangles (V-shaped torso, pointed features) reads as threatening.

Secondary shapes: major body section shapes that add character within the primary silhouette. Broad pauldrons, a flowing cape, oversized boots. These are visible at medium distance and define the character's role (warrior, mage, rogue).

Tertiary shapes: fine surface detail visible only at close range. Belt buckles, stitching patterns, jewelry, weapon engravings. These add richness for cutscenes and character select screens but are invisible during gameplay.

Shape hierarchy consistency: all three levels should reinforce the same personality. Mixing soft primary shapes with sharp tertiary details creates visual confusion. Consistency creates characters that read correctly at every distance.

Turnaround views: front, 3/4, side, and back at consistent scale on a proportion grid. Turnarounds eliminate guesswork for the 3D modeler by defining every angle explicitly. The proportion grid ensures measurements are consistent across views.

Expression sheet: 6-8 facial expressions showing the character's emotional range (neutral, happy, angry, sad, surprised, determined, fearful, contemptuous). Expression sheets define the target for facial animation blend shapes.

Costume and prop callouts: zoomed-in detail panels showing accessories, weapons, and material properties that are not clearly visible in the turnaround views. Include material notes (leather, metal, fabric) for the texture artist.

Color palette specification: exact hex/RGB values for skin, hair, clothing primary, clothing secondary, and accent colors. Vague color descriptions ('blueish') cause inconsistency; exact values prevent it.

Scale reference: the character shown alongside other characters and environmental objects to establish relative size. A character described as 'tall' needs a specific centimeter height and a comparison figure.

Production cost awareness: every shape detail has a polygon cost, a texture cost, and an animation cost. Level 3 detail that is invisible during gameplay is wasted production budget. The model sheet should annotate which details are essential vs optional.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating a complete character model sheet in a live drawing session: starting from a rough concept (a friendly blacksmith NPC), establishing primary circle-based shapes, adding secondary shapes (broad chest, rolled sleeves, leather apron), adding tertiary detail callouts (tool loops, forge burn scars, wedding ring), and placing turnaround views on a proportion grid.
setup_requirements: Digital drawing application with layer support
Proportion grid template (provided in course materials)
Color picker and swatch palette tool
Reference images of professional character model sheets

review.value: Primary (overall silhouette), secondary (body section shapes), tertiary (surface detail). All three levels should communicate the same personality. A friendly character has round shapes at every level. A threatening character has angular shapes at every level.

Front, side, 3/4, and back drawings at identical scale on a proportion grid. The 3D modeler uses these as blueprints for mesh construction. Inconsistent scale or missing angles force guesswork that introduces errors.

A grid of 6-8 head drawings showing key emotions. These define the target for facial animation blend shapes. The expressions must cover the character's emotional range for their role: a comedy sidekick needs exaggerated expressions; a stoic warrior needs subtle ones.

Every tertiary detail costs polygons, texture space, and animation support. A belt buckle invisible during gameplay is wasted budget. The model sheet should annotate detail levels: 'essential for gameplay readability' vs 'nice-to-have for cutscenes.'

learning_objectives: Construct character personality profiles using the motivation triad (want, need, fear) that drive consistent behavior
Design character motivations that create meaningful player investment and emotional engagement
Write character dialogue that reveals personality through voice rather than exposition
Apply the personality diamond framework to define behavioral boundaries for interactive characters
readings: The Art of Game Design Ch. 21 (Jesse Schell)
Writing for Video Games Ch. 4-6 (Steve Ince)
Character and Viewpoint Ch. 1-3 (Orson Scott Card)

lecture_notes.introduction: Visual design gets the player's attention. Personality keeps it. A character's motivation -- what they want, what they fear, what they are willing to sacrifice -- is the engine that drives player investment. In passive media, a character's personality is expressed through the writer's chosen actions and dialogue. In games, the character must also express personality through systemic behavior: how they react to the player's choices, what they do when the player is not interacting with them, and how they handle failure. This requires a deep understanding of the character that goes beyond a backstory paragraph. Today we build personality profiles using two frameworks: the motivation triad (want, need, fear) that defines the character's internal conflict, and the personality diamond (warmth/coldness, confidence/doubt, idealism/pragmatism, introversion/extroversion) that defines their behavioral range. Together, these tools generate consistent character behavior across thousands of possible game situations without the designer scripting every response individually.
key_concepts: The motivation triad: want (conscious goal the character pursues), need (unconscious flaw they must overcome), fear (the consequence of failure). The tension between want and need creates depth because pursuing the want without addressing the need leads to failure.

Character voice: vocabulary, sentence structure, speech patterns, and verbal tics that make dialogue attributable without a name tag. A grizzled soldier uses short, declarative sentences. A nervous scholar uses long, hedging sentences. Good voice is identifiable in a blind reading.

Show-don't-tell in interactive media: reveal personality through actions, reactions, and choices rather than exposition dumps. A character who silently gives their last health potion to the player communicates generosity more powerfully than dialogue saying 'I am generous.'

Character arcs in games: how player agency interacts with authored character growth. In linear narratives, the writer controls the arc. In branching narratives, the player influences the arc. In systemic games, the arc emerges from gameplay.

The personality diamond: four axes (warmth/coldness, confidence/doubt, idealism/pragmatism, introversion/extroversion) that define a character's behavioral space. Each axis is a spectrum, not binary. A character at 0.7 warmth and 0.3 confidence behaves differently from one at 0.3 warmth and 0.7 confidence.

Dialogue as character revelation: every line of dialogue should serve at least two purposes: advancing the plot AND revealing something about the character's personality. Exposition-only dialogue wastes the opportunity.

Consistent inconsistency: real people have contradictions (a brave warrior afraid of spiders). One carefully chosen contradiction makes a character feel human. Too many contradictions make them incoherent.

Backstory as behavior driver: backstory is not interesting in itself. It is interesting because it explains current behavior. A character's fear of fire is compelling if it causes them to hesitate during a burning building rescue. Without behavioral impact, backstory is trivia.

Player-character relationship design: the emotional bond between the player and a game character is built through shared experience (surviving a battle together), mutual aid (the character saves the player), and vulnerability (the character reveals a weakness).

Writing for localization: character voice must survive translation. Puns, slang, and culture-specific references may not translate. Writing characters with personality traits that transcend language (bravery, humor, kindness) ensures global appeal.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building personality profiles for three characters for a hypothetical RPG: a mentor with Hero+Shadow archetype blend (want: train the hero, need: overcome guilt for past failure, fear: the hero repeating their mistakes), an antagonist with Ruler+Sage blend, and a companion with Innocent+Rebel blend. Then writing three dialogue exchanges where personality differences drive the conflict.
setup_requirements: Character profile template (provided in course materials)
Word processor or screenwriting software
Personality diamond worksheet

review.value: Want: what the character consciously pursues (find the artifact). Need: what they must actually learn or overcome (trust others). Fear: what happens if they fail (becoming like the villain). The tension between want and need creates depth and drives character growth.

A character's unique speech pattern. A grizzled soldier uses short declarative sentences with military jargon. A nervous scholar uses long hedging sentences with academic vocabulary. Good character voice means the reader can identify the speaker without attribution.

Four axes defining behavioral range: warmth/coldness (how they treat others), confidence/doubt (how they face challenges), idealism/pragmatism (how they make decisions), introversion/extroversion (how they engage socially). A character at specific points on these axes has predictable behavioral tendencies.

Backstory only matters if it drives current behavior. A character's traumatic past is irrelevant if it does not influence their actions in the game. Every backstory element should manifest as a visible behavioral pattern the player can observe and understand.

learning_objectives: Apply the 12 principles of animation to game character movement with emphasis on gameplay communication
Understand how animation style communicates personality as strongly as visual design
Design idle animations that express character personality through subtle body language
Balance animation quality with gameplay responsiveness and input latency
readings: The Animator's Survival Kit Ch. 1-3 (Richard Williams)
Game Anim: Video Game Animation Explained Ch. 4-6 (Jonathan Cooper)
GDC 2016: 'Animation Bootcamp: An Indie Approach to Animation' (David Rosen)

lecture_notes.introduction: Animation is where a character truly comes alive. A character can look perfect in a T-pose and feel lifeless in motion. The way a character walks, idles, reacts, and emotes defines their personality as much as their dialogue. In film animation, the 12 principles (squash and stretch, anticipation, staging, etc.) serve aesthetic goals: making movement feel natural, weighty, and appealing. In games, these same principles serve a dual purpose: aesthetic quality AND gameplay communication. Anticipation frames on an enemy attack are not just visually satisfying -- they are the player's reaction window. Without them, the attack feels unfair. Squash and stretch on a jump makes it feel bouncy and fun, but it also communicates the arc trajectory so the player can judge landing precision. Today we study how each animation principle serves both aesthetics and gameplay, and practice designing animations that balance visual quality with the responsiveness demands of real-time interactive control.
key_concepts: The 12 principles of animation applied to games: squash and stretch (impact feedback), anticipation (attack telegraphing), staging (camera framing for readability), follow-through (weapon and hair settling), slow in/slow out (acceleration curves), arcs (natural movement paths), secondary action (cape and equipment physics), timing (frame count determines gameplay feel), exaggeration (readability at distance), solid drawing (consistent volume), appeal (likeable motion quality), straight ahead/pose-to-pose (workflow methods).

Anticipation as gameplay telegraph: a preparatory movement before the main action (pulling back before a punch, crouching before a jump). In games, anticipation frames are the player's entire reaction window. More frames = easier; fewer frames = harder.

Idle animations as personality statements: how a character stands when the player is not moving reveals who they are. A warrior shifts weight and checks their weapon. A scholar adjusts their glasses and glances around. A child fidgets and plays.

Weight and gravity: animation must communicate the character's mass. A heavy character's footsteps shake the camera and their turns are slow. A light character bounces between steps and changes direction instantly. Weight determines gameplay feel.

Animation canceling and responsiveness: long, beautiful animations feel terrible if they lock the player out of control. Games balance quality with responsiveness by allowing animation canceling (interrupting one animation to start another) at specific cancel windows.
Blend trees: blending between walk, jog, and run animations based on analog stick input. The blend must be seamless -- any visible 'pop' between animations breaks the illusion of continuous movement.

Root motion vs script-driven motion: root motion moves the character based on the animation clip's displacement, ensuring feet do not slide. Script-driven motion moves the character programmatically and plays matching animations, offering more control but risking foot sliding.

Animation state machines: connecting idle, locomotion, combat, and emote animation layers with transitions. Each state has entry/exit animations, and blending between states prevents jarring visual cuts.

Hit reactions and hurt animations: visual feedback when a character takes damage. Varied hit reactions (stagger, flinch, knockback, knockdown) based on damage type communicate combat impact and help the player prioritize threats.

Animation budget: the number of unique animations a character requires. A basic character needs 30-50 animations (idle, walk, run, jump, fall, land, attack, hurt, die). A premium character in a AAA game may have 200+.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing three characters' walk cycles to identify how animation style communicates personality: a confident soldier (long strides, upright posture, minimal arm swing), a nervous thief (quick steps, hunched shoulders, darting head turns), and a playful child (bouncy steps, wide arm swings, occasional skipping). Then blocking out a 4-pose walk cycle key frames in Blender.
setup_requirements: Blender 3.6+ with provided rigged character model
Reference animation clips for analysis
Understanding of keyframe animation and timeline tools

review.value: A preparatory movement in the opposite direction before the main action. In games, anticipation frames are attack telegraphs that give players reaction time. A boss raising their sword for 20 frames before swinging gives the player a readable dodge window.

Idle animations reveal character when the player is passive. A warrior checks their weapon and scans for threats. A scholar reads a book they pull from their pocket. These animations run on a timer (10-30 seconds of base idle, then a personality idle plays).

Allowing the player to interrupt one animation to start another at defined cancel windows. Without canceling, long attack animations lock the player out of dodge inputs, making combat feel unresponsive. Cancel points must be tuned to balance responsiveness with commitment.

Root motion: character position comes from the animation clip, ensuring feet plant correctly. Script-driven: character position comes from code, animation is cosmetic. Root motion prevents foot sliding but limits programmatic control. Many games use root motion for combat and script-driven for locomotion.

learning_objectives: Understand the Facial Action Coding System and map Action Units to blend shapes for real-time facial animation
Design facial expression libraries that cover the emotional range needed for interactive characters
Create blend shape combinations that produce naturalistic expressions without uncanny valley artifacts
Apply FACS principles to stylized characters where anatomical fidelity is secondary to readability
readings: Game Anim: Video Game Animation Explained Ch. 7-8 (Jonathan Cooper)
Facial Action Coding System (Ekman & Friesen) summary document
GDC 2018: 'Facial Animation in God of War' (Joel Isaac)

lecture_notes.introduction: The human face is the most expressive surface on earth. We are biologically wired to read faces -- a baby recognizes their mother's face before they can focus on anything else. In games, facial animation determines whether a character feels alive or dead behind the eyes. The Facial Action Coding System (FACS), developed by psychologists Paul Ekman and Wallace Friesen, catalogs every visible facial movement as an Action Unit (AU) corresponding to a specific facial muscle contraction. In game production, each AU becomes a blend shape (also called morph target or shape key): a stored mesh deformation that can be blended additively with others. Combining AU1 (inner brow raise) + AU4 (brow lowerer) + AU15 (lip corner depressor) produces sadness. The power of FACS is composability: 30-52 blend shapes can produce thousands of distinct expressions through combination. Today we study the FACS system, map it to game production blend shapes, and practice creating expressions in Blender.
key_concepts: Facial Action Coding System (FACS): 46 Action Units corresponding to individual facial muscle movements. Each AU has a specific anatomical basis (AU1 = frontalis pars medialis = inner brow raise).

Core blend shapes for games: a minimum viable set of 30 blend shapes covers the essential AUs. A full set of 52 shapes covers the complete FACS catalog including tongue and jaw articulation for lipsync.

Additive blending: blend shapes combine additively -- AU1 (inner brow raise) at 50% + AU2 (outer brow raise) at 70% produces a specific surprised-brow shape. The animator controls each AU independently as a 0-1 slider.

Emotion recipes: happiness = AU6 (cheek raise) + AU12 (lip corner pull). Sadness = AU1 + AU4 + AU15. Anger = AU4 + AU5 (upper lid raise) + AU23 (lip tightener). Fear = AU1 + AU2 + AU4 + AU5 + AU20 (lip stretch).

The uncanny valley in facial animation: when a character's face is almost realistic but slightly wrong, it triggers revulsion rather than empathy. Common causes: asymmetric deformation, dead eyes (no micro-movements), and smile shapes that do not reach the eyes (Duchenne smile requires AU6).

Micro-expressions: brief involuntary facial movements lasting 1/25th to 1/5th of a second that reveal concealed emotions. In games, adding subtle micro-expressions to NPCs during dialogue creates the impression of hidden depth.

Eye animation: eye direction, blink rate, and pupil dilation contribute enormously to perceived life. A character who does not blink or whose eyes do not track the conversation feels dead. Procedural eye animation with saccades (quick eye darts) and periodic blinks adds life cheaply.

Lipsync: mapping phonemes (speech sounds) to visemes (mouth shapes). A minimum set of 15 visemes covers English speech. Real-time lipsync systems analyze audio waveforms and drive viseme blend shapes procedurally.

Stylized facial animation: cartoony characters do not follow FACS literally but use exaggerated versions of the same AUs. Larger brow movements, wider mouths, and asymmetric expressions create appeal in stylized characters.

Performance capture overview: recording actors' facial movements with marker-based or markerless camera systems and retargeting the performance onto game characters. Mo-cap provides naturalistic foundation but requires cleanup and exaggeration for game readability.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Rigging a character face in Blender with 8 core FACS blend shapes (brow raise inner AU1, brow lower AU4, eye squint AU6, eye wide AU5, smile AU12, frown AU15, jaw open AU26, lip pucker AU18). Combining blend shapes to create happiness, sadness, anger, and surprise. Then driving expressions with a simple emotion controller.
setup_requirements: Blender 3.6+ with a rigged character head model (provided in course materials)
Understanding of mesh deformation and shape keys in Blender
FACS Action Unit reference chart (Ekman reference set)
Expression reference photos for each core emotion

review.value: The Facial Action Coding System defines 46 Action Units corresponding to facial muscle movements. In games, 30-52 blend shapes modeled from these AUs are combined additively to produce any facial expression. Happiness = AU6 (cheek raise) + AU12 (lip corner pull).

When a character's face is 90% realistic but 10% wrong, the result is more disturbing than a clearly stylized face. Common causes: dead eyes, asymmetric deformation, smiles without AU6 (cheek raise), and missing micro-movements. Stylization avoids the uncanny valley by not attempting realism.

Eyes are the first thing players look at. A character whose eyes do not blink, do not track conversation partners, and do not perform saccades (quick darts between focus points) feels lifeless. Procedural eye animation is cheap and dramatically increases perceived life.

A genuine smile contracts both the lip corners (AU12) and the cheeks around the eyes (AU6). A fake smile only uses AU12 -- the 'polite smile.' Characters whose smiles do not reach their eyes appear insincere or creepy. AU6 is the difference between a character feeling warm and feeling untrustworthy.

learning_objectives: Understand the motion capture pipeline from studio recording to in-engine retargeting
Evaluate when motion capture is appropriate vs hand-animated keyframe animation
Identify common motion capture artifacts and the cleanup process required for game-ready data
Apply motion capture data to stylized characters through retargeting and post-capture exaggeration
readings: Game Anim Ch. 9-10 (Jonathan Cooper)
Understanding Motion Capture for Computer Animation (Alberto Menache) Ch. 1-3
GDC 2017: 'Motion Capture at Naughty Dog' (Jeremy Yates)

lecture_notes.introduction: Motion capture records real human movement and transfers it to digital characters. When it works well, mo-cap provides a foundation of naturalistic movement that would take keyframe animators weeks to reproduce. When it works poorly, it produces data that is noisy, requires extensive cleanup, and loses the exaggeration and clarity that hand-animation provides. The decision to use mo-cap depends on the project's visual style (realistic characters benefit more than stylized ones), budget (mo-cap sessions are expensive), and animation volume (a game with 2000+ unique animations amortizes the setup cost better than one with 50). Today we study the complete mo-cap pipeline: studio setup, actor direction, data capture, cleanup, retargeting to game characters, and the post-capture process where animators adjust the raw data for gameplay responsiveness and visual polish. Understanding mo-cap is essential even if you never direct a session yourself, because modern game animation pipelines frequently blend mo-cap foundation with hand-keyed adjustments.
key_concepts: Optical marker-based capture: reflective markers placed on the actor's body and face are tracked by infrared cameras. Marker positions are reconstructed into 3D skeleton data. This is the most common method for body capture in games.

Markerless capture: computer vision algorithms track the actor's body and face from video without physical markers. Less accurate but cheaper and faster to set up. Increasingly viable with machine learning improvements.

Performance capture: simultaneous recording of body motion, facial expressions, and voice acting. Enables actors to deliver integrated performances where facial expressions match body language and dialogue timing.

Retargeting: mapping motion capture data from the actor's skeleton to the game character's skeleton. Requires matching bone hierarchies and adjusting for proportion differences (the actor's arm length vs the character's).

Mo-cap cleanup: raw capture data contains noise (jitter), occlusion gaps (missing marker data when a limb is hidden), and ground contact errors (feet sliding). Cleanup is typically 2-3x the capture session duration.

Actor direction for games: game animation requires exaggerated performances because subtlety is lost at gameplay camera distance. Directors must guide actors to perform larger, clearer movements than they would for film.

Mo-cap for stylized characters: retargeting realistic mo-cap onto cartoony characters requires post-capture exaggeration -- amplifying movements, adding squash and stretch, and adjusting timing to match the character's visual style.

Mo-cap vs keyframe: mo-cap excels at natural locomotion, dialogue performances, and realistic interactions. Keyframe excels at stylized movement, combat with precise timing requirements, and fantastical actions (magic casting, flying).

Cost-benefit analysis: a mo-cap session costs $10,000-$50,000 but produces 100+ animation clips. Keyframing one clip costs $500-$1,500 in animator time. For projects with 200+ animations, mo-cap breaks even around 20-50 clips.

Motion libraries: commercial motion capture libraries (Mixamo, Rokoko, Move.ai) provide pre-recorded animations that can be retargeted to custom characters, reducing costs for indie and mid-size studios.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Viewing raw motion capture data alongside the cleaned-up version to identify noise, occlusion artifacts, and foot sliding. Then retargeting a Mixamo motion capture clip onto a custom Blender character, adjusting the retargeting map for proportion differences, and exaggerating the movement for a stylized aesthetic.
setup_requirements: Blender 3.6+ with Mixamo animation retargeting add-on
Provided raw and cleaned mo-cap data files (BVH format)
Custom character rig from previous sessions
Understanding of skeleton hierarchies and bone constraints

review.value: Mapping motion data from one skeleton to another. The capture actor's proportions rarely match the game character's. Retargeting adjusts bone positions proportionally while preserving the motion's essence. Poor retargeting causes feet to float, arms to clip through bodies, or fingers to stretch.

Raw capture data has noise (jitter from marker wobble), gaps (occluded markers), and ground contact errors (foot sliding). Cleanup typically takes 2-3x the capture session duration. Skipping cleanup produces visibly flawed in-game animation.

Use mo-cap when: realistic style, large animation volume (200+), performance-driven scenes. Use keyframe when: stylized style, precise gameplay timing, fantastical actions, small animation volume. Most AAA games use both: mo-cap foundation with keyframe adjustments.

Recording body, face, and voice simultaneously allows integrated performances where facial expressions naturally match dialogue and body language. This produces the most naturalistic results but requires actors trained in mo-cap performance and expensive multi-system capture studios.

learning_objectives: Design NPCs that feel like inhabitants of the game world rather than stationary quest dispensers
Create NPC behavior layers (ambient, reactive, interactive) that produce the illusion of daily life
Implement daily schedule systems that make the world feel alive without requiring expensive unique content for every NPC
Balance NPC density and behavior complexity against performance budgets
readings: AI for Games Ch. 7 (Ian Millington)
GDC 2018: 'Building a Living World in Red Dead Redemption 2' (Rob Nelson)
The Art of Game Design Ch. 23 (Jesse Schell)

lecture_notes.introduction: NPCs are the population of your game world. Bad NPCs break immersion faster than any graphical flaw. A shopkeeper who stares at a wall, a guard who ignores a murder committed in front of them, a quest-giver who repeats the same greeting for the hundredth time -- these are design failures that reduce a living world to a cardboard stage. Great NPC design creates the illusion that every character has a life beyond the player's immediate needs. They sleep, eat, work, socialize, and react to the world's state. This illusion does not require simulating complete lives for every NPC -- it requires carefully designed behavior layers that maximize perceived life while minimizing computational and content cost. Today we study NPC behavior design as a character design discipline: each NPC's behavior should be an extension of their personality, serving both gameplay function and world-building atmosphere.
key_concepts: NPC behavior layers: ambient (idle activities, daily routines), reactive (respond to player actions and world events), interactive (conversation, trading, questing). Each layer adds perceived depth but also adds development cost.

Daily schedule systems: NPCs with routines (sleep at home 10pm-6am, walk to work 6am-7am, work at shop 7am-6pm, eat at tavern 6pm-9pm, walk home 9pm-10pm). Schedules make the world feel alive and teach the player where to find NPCs.

Ambient behavior variety: within a schedule block (e.g., 'working at the forge'), NPCs perform varied activities (hammering, inspecting tools, wiping brow, drinking water, chatting with a customer). Variation prevents the robotic feeling of a single looped animation.

Reactive behavior: NPCs respond to game state changes. If it rains, they seek shelter. If the player draws a weapon, nearby NPCs flee or prepare to fight. If a fire starts, they evacuate. Reactive behavior creates the illusion of awareness.

World-state reactions: NPCs remember and react to the player's actions. Helping a town makes its citizens friendlier. Stealing makes them suspicious. These reputation-based behavioral changes create consequence for player choices.

NPC barks: short contextual voice lines (2-8 seconds) triggered by game state rather than player interaction. 'Nice weather today,' 'Watch your step,' 'Did you hear about the bandit attack?' Barks create the sense of a living community.

Performance budgeting: in a town with 50 NPCs, full behavior simulation for all of them is expensive. Strategies: only simulate NPCs within a radius of the player, use simplified behavior for distant NPCs, and teleport off-screen NPCs to their schedule positions.

Crowd systems: for large populations (city streets, battlefields), individual behavior is replaced with crowd flows -- groups of NPCs moving along predefined paths with variation injected through random stops, conversations, and reactions.

NPC personality archetypes: a small set of personality templates (friendly merchant, suspicious guard, curious child, weary traveler) that can be applied to many NPCs with visual and voice variation to create diversity from reusable components.

Failure states: what happens when NPC behavior breaks? An NPC stuck on geometry, walking into a wall, or standing in the rain without reacting. Graceful failure handling (timeout to reset, teleport to safe position, fallback to idle) prevents immersion-breaking bugs.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing an NPC behavior system for a small village: 8 NPCs with daily schedules, 3 ambient behaviors per schedule block, reactive behaviors for rain and player proximity, and 5 contextual barks per NPC. Implementing a simplified version in a behavior tree editor to demonstrate the layer structure.
setup_requirements: Behavior tree editor (Unreal Engine or custom tool)
Village scene with 8 NPC positions and schedule waypoints
Spreadsheet for bark script and schedule organization
Basic NPC AI controller with navigation

review.value: Ambient (what the NPC does by default), reactive (how they respond to the world), interactive (how they respond to the player). Each layer adds depth but costs development time. Budget layers based on the NPC's importance: quest-critical NPCs get all three; background NPCs get ambient only.

NPCs that eat, sleep, and work on a schedule make the world feel alive. The player learns to find the blacksmith at the forge during the day and at the tavern in the evening. Schedules also create gameplay opportunities: rob the empty shop while the owner sleeps.

Only fully simulate NPCs the player can see. Distant NPCs use simplified state (teleport to schedule position). Off-screen NPCs freeze behavior entirely. When the player approaches, resume from the expected schedule state. The player never sees the shortcut.

NPC behavior will break. NPCs will get stuck on geometry, walk through doors, or stand in fire. Graceful failure handling (teleport to safe position after 10 seconds of pathfinding failure, fallback to idle animation) prevents the bug from being immersion-breaking.

learning_objectives: Design companion AI that enhances gameplay without becoming a liability or stealing player agency
Apply the four companion design pillars: never block, never steal, always help, always feel present
Create contextual bark scripts that make companions feel aware and responsive to the game world
Balance companion utility with the player's sense of accomplishment and control
readings: GDC 2014: 'Building Companion AI in The Last of Us' (Max Dyckhoff)
GDC 2020: 'Companion AI Design in God of War' (Mihir Sheth)
AI for Games Ch. 8 (Ian Millington)

lecture_notes.introduction: Companion characters are the hardest AI design challenge in games. They must be useful without being overpowered, present without being annoying, helpful without stealing the player's accomplishments, and smart without exposing the limitations of game AI. A companion who blocks doorways, runs into crossfire, steals kills, or repeats the same line every 30 seconds is worse than no companion at all. The gold standard is Elizabeth from BioShock Infinite: she tosses the player ammo and health, comments on the environment, reacts to events, and never gets in the way. She makes the player feel like they have a partner, not a burden. The key design insight is that companions must be designed from the player's perspective, not the companion's. Every behavior should answer the question: 'Does this make the player's experience better?' If the answer is no, cut the behavior. Today we study companion design principles, create behavior state machines for companions, and write contextual barks that make the companion feel like a real presence in the world.
key_concepts: The four companion pillars: (1) never block the player's movement path, (2) never steal kills or glory moments, (3) always provide useful assistance (resources, combat support, information), (4) always feel present through environmental interaction and contextual dialogue.

The Elizabeth principle (BioShock Infinite): companions should enhance gameplay through environmental interaction, resource sharing, and emotional presence without ever being a liability. The player should never have to babysit or work around their companion.

Companion positioning: the companion maintains a 'follow bubble' -- close enough to feel present, far enough to not obstruct. In combat, they find cover near the player. In corridors, they yield doorways. In exploration, they wander slightly to examine objects.

Combat support design: companions should assist combat in ways that complement but never replace the player's contributions. They can suppress enemies (keeping them behind cover), weaken enemies (chip damage), or provide tactical information ('one behind you!').

Kill credit: when a companion kills an enemy, the player should still feel like they contributed. Techniques: companions only finish enemies the player has already damaged, companions focus on secondary threats while the player handles primary targets.

Contextual barks: short voice lines triggered by game state. Categories: combat ('reloading!'), exploration ('look at this'), ambient ('wonder what happened here'), reaction ('that was close'), idle ('nice view from here'). 50-100 barks with cooldown timers prevent repetition.

Emotional presence: the companion reacts to the world the way a real person would -- commenting on beautiful vistas, expressing discomfort in dark places, showing concern when the player is hurt, and celebrating victories. These reactions build emotional bond.

Companion behavior state machine: Follow (default, maintain follow distance), Combat (find cover, engage secondary targets, provide call-outs), Explore (wander to nearby points of interest, interact with objects, wait for player), Scripted (cutscene and quest-specific behaviors).

Player attachment building: shared adversity (surviving a difficult battle together), mutual aid (the companion saves the player in a scripted moment), and vulnerability (the companion reveals a fear or weakness) all build the player-companion emotional bond.

Companion customization: allowing the player to adjust companion behavior (aggressive/passive, chatty/quiet, close follow/loose follow) accommodates different player preferences without requiring the designer to pick one default.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a companion character from personality through behavior: defining their personality diamond (warm, confident, idealistic, extroverted), writing 20 contextual barks across 5 trigger categories, and mapping their behavior state machine with transitions between Follow, Combat, Explore, and Scripted states.
setup_requirements: Behavior state machine editor or diagramming tool
Character profile from earlier sessions
Spreadsheet template for bark script organization
Reference videos of companion AI in published games

review.value: The companion enhances the player's experience through emotional presence and occasional gameplay aid without ever becoming a burden. The player should never babysit, protect, or work around their companion. If the companion causes a single player death through their behavior, the design has failed.

Short voice lines triggered by game state rather than player interaction. A companion saying 'looks like rain' when weather changes or 'careful, I hear something' near enemies creates awareness. 50-100 barks with 30-60 second cooldown timers prevent repetition.

The companion maintains a dynamic distance from the player: close in tight corridors, wider in open areas, behind cover during combat. The bubble adjusts to context so the companion feels present without being obstructive. Doorway yielding is the most critical behavior.

The player bonds with a companion through: shared adversity (surviving together), mutual aid (companion saves player in a scripted moment), vulnerability (companion admits a fear), and consistent presence (companion reacts to the world alongside the player). Bonds build through experience, not exposition.

learning_objectives: Design boss encounters that test specific skills taught throughout the game while introducing one new challenge
Create multi-phase boss fights with escalating difficulty and distinct visual/mechanical identities per phase
Apply character design principles to boss visual design for maximum impact and readability
Balance boss difficulty through telegraph timing, pattern complexity, and recovery window design
readings: The Art of Game Design Ch. 24 (Jesse Schell)
GDC 2019: 'Designing Boss Fights in Sekiro' (Masanori Aoki)
Boss Fight Books: Shovel Knight (David L. Craddock) Ch. 7-9

lecture_notes.introduction: A boss fight is the final exam of a game section. Everything the player has learned -- mechanics, enemy patterns, environmental awareness, resource management -- is tested in a single climactic encounter. But a great boss fight is not just a skill check; it is a character moment. The boss is a character the player has been anticipating, fearing, or hating throughout the level. Their visual design, attack animations, dialogue, and arena all contribute to an encounter that feels like confronting a person, not solving a puzzle. The best boss fights in gaming history (Ornstein and Smough, Mr. Freeze, Psycho Mantis, Sephiroth) are remembered not for their mechanical difficulty but for the way the character design elevated the encounter into something memorable. Today we study boss design as the intersection of character design, level design, and combat design, creating encounters where the boss's personality drives both their visual identity and their attack patterns.
key_concepts: Boss as skill test: each boss should primarily test one or two skills the player has been developing. A boss that tests everything simultaneously is overwhelming; a boss that tests nothing new is anticlimactic.

Multi-phase design: 2-3 distinct phases with escalating difficulty. Each phase introduces one new mechanic while retaining core patterns. Phase transitions are marked by visual changes (damaged armor, new weapon, transformation) that communicate the shift.

Attack pattern legibility: every boss attack must be readable through animation telegraphs, audio cues, and visual effects. The player should be able to learn the pattern through observation, not trial-and-error death.

Recovery windows: moments after a boss attack where the boss is vulnerable. These reward the player for learning the pattern: avoid the attack, then punish during recovery. The duration of recovery windows controls difficulty.

Boss visual design: bosses should be the most visually impressive characters in the game. Larger scale, unique silhouette, distinctive color palette, and exaggerated animations all communicate 'this is important.' The visual design should hint at their attack types.

Arena design for bosses: the arena is part of the boss encounter. A circular arena enables 360-degree movement. Pillars provide cover for projectile-heavy bosses. Destructible elements escalate the encounter by removing safe zones.

Difficulty through phases, not health pools: a boss with 50,000 HP and one attack pattern is a damage sponge. A boss with 15,000 HP across 3 phases with distinct mechanics is a compelling encounter. Each phase should feel like a new challenge.

Boss personality in combat: the boss's personality should be visible in their fighting style. An arrogant boss attacks recklessly and taunts. A calculating boss uses traps and environmental hazards. A desperate boss becomes more dangerous as their health drops.

Narrative closure: defeating a boss should resolve a narrative thread. The boss's death animation, final dialogue, or environmental change should provide emotional payoff for the player's investment in the conflict.

Optional challenge bosses: hidden bosses with significantly higher difficulty that reward skilled players with unique loot, lore, or achievements. These encounters are designed for the top 10-20% of players and are separate from the main progression.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a three-phase boss fight on paper: Phase 1 -- the boss uses melee attacks with clear telegraphs, teaching the player the dodge timing. Phase 2 -- the boss adds ranged attacks and arena hazards, requiring the player to manage positioning. Phase 3 -- the boss becomes faster and the arena shrinks, creating a frantic finale. Visual design evolves across phases: armor pieces break off, revealing a more vulnerable but more dangerous form.
setup_requirements: Boss design document template (provided in course materials)
Drawing materials for boss concept sketches
Reference videos of analyzed boss encounters
Attack pattern timeline template for phase planning

review.value: 2-3 phases with escalating difficulty and distinct mechanics. Phase transitions are marked by visual and mechanical changes. Each phase adds one new element while retaining core patterns. This creates an escalation arc within the encounter.

After each boss attack pattern, there must be a window where the player can safely deal damage. The duration of recovery windows is the primary difficulty dial: longer windows = easier, shorter windows = harder. No recovery windows = unfair.

The boss's personality drives their fighting style. An arrogant boss has showy attacks with long telegraphs (overconfident). A cunning boss uses traps and terrain. A desperate boss becomes erratic and faster at low health. Personality makes the encounter feel like fighting a character, not a health bar.

The arena is part of the boss design. A fire boss fights in a room with lava that rises in phase 3. A wind boss fights on a rooftop where gusts push the player toward edges. The arena amplifies the boss's personality and creates environmental challenges.

learning_objectives: Design character customization systems that allow player expression while maintaining visual cohesion
Understand the technical requirements of modular character systems (mesh swapping, texture atlases, material instances)
Balance customization depth with production cost and performance constraints
Prevent customization from undermining gameplay readability (friend/foe identification, class identification)
readings: GDC 2018: 'Character Customization in Destiny 2' (Mike Zak)
The Art of Game Design Ch. 25 (Jesse Schell)
GDC 2020: 'Inclusive Character Creation' (Raquel Robinson)

lecture_notes.introduction: Character customization is one of the most requested features in games. Players want to express their identity through their avatar: their appearance, clothing, accessories, and equipment should reflect their personal aesthetic. But customization creates design tension: the more freedom players have, the harder it is to maintain visual quality, performance, and gameplay readability. Allowing players to wear any combination of armor pieces means that pieces designed by different artists may clash visually. Allowing any color combination means some players will create visually offensive or unreadable characters. Allowing full body proportion adjustment means animations may deform incorrectly on extreme body types. Today we study customization as a design problem: how to maximize player expression while maintaining the visual quality and gameplay clarity that makes the game function. The solution is not unlimited freedom but curated freedom -- providing enough options that every player feels represented while constraining the system to prevent visual chaos.
key_concepts: Modular character systems: characters assembled from interchangeable body parts (head, torso, arms, legs) and equipment pieces (helmet, chest, gloves, boots) that snap together on a shared skeleton.

Mesh compatibility: all modular pieces must deform correctly on the shared skeleton. Shoulder pieces must not clip through chest pieces. Helmets must fit all head shapes. This requires strict modeling guidelines and extensive QA.
Texture atlases: combining multiple character texture maps onto a single atlas to reduce draw calls. Customization pieces share a common UV layout so any piece can use the same atlas slot.

Material instance customization: allowing players to change the color, roughness, and pattern of equipment without creating new textures. Material instances are cheap to render because they share the same shader but with different parameter values.

Preset systems: pre-designed character looks (class fantasies, cultural outfits, signature styles) that players can use as-is or modify. Presets ensure visual quality as a baseline while customization adds personal expression.

Body type inclusivity: offering diverse body types (body shapes, heights, proportions) with appropriate animation retargeting. Animations must look natural on all available body types, not just the default.

Facial feature diversity: skin tones, facial structures, hairstyles, and accessories that represent diverse ethnicities, ages, and gender expressions. Consultation with cultural representatives prevents harmful stereotypes.

Readability constraints: in PvP games, customization must not prevent friend/foe identification. Solutions include faction-colored elements that cannot be changed, class-distinctive silhouettes that persist across outfits, and enemy outline highlights.

Customization UI design: presenting hundreds of options without overwhelming the player. Category-based browsing (hair, face, body, clothing), preview rendering, randomize buttons, and save/load presets reduce decision fatigue.

Monetization considerations: cosmetic customization is the primary monetization model for free-to-play games. Designing attractive but non-essential cosmetics that do not affect gameplay balance is an art that intersects with character design.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a modular character customization system on paper: defining the body slot taxonomy (6 equipment slots, 4 body customization categories), establishing visual constraints (faction colors, class silhouettes), and creating 3 preset characters that demonstrate the system's range. Then reviewing a character customization UI flow for usability.
setup_requirements: Character customization design document template
Reference screenshots from published character creators
UI wireframing tool for customization interface design
Diversity reference guide (provided in course materials)

review.value: Effective customization provides enough options for personal expression while constraining the system to prevent visual chaos. Color palettes instead of color wheels, pre-designed hairstyles instead of vertex editors, and equipment sets instead of individual polygon editing.

All interchangeable pieces must work together without clipping, deformation errors, or visual mismatches. This requires strict modeling guidelines: consistent UV layouts, shared bone weights at connection points, and tolerance for material variation.

In multiplayer games, customization must not prevent class or faction identification. Overwatch preserves class readability by maintaining each hero's distinctive silhouette regardless of skin. Faction identification uses persistent colored elements.

Character creators should represent diverse body types, skin tones, facial structures, hairstyles, and gender expressions. This is not just ethical design -- it expands the game's market by ensuring more players see themselves represented.

learning_objectives: Design character representations for UI contexts: portraits, inventory screens, dialogue boxes, and HUD elements
Understand how UI character art serves different functions than in-game character rendering
Create consistent character representation across gameplay, menus, marketing, and social media
Apply character personality to UI interactions (menu animations, loading screens, system messages)
readings: Game UI Design (Kevin Saunders) Ch. 8-9
GDC 2019: 'UI Character Design in Persona 5' (Masayoshi Suto)
The Design of Everyday Things Ch. 1-2 (Don Norman) applied to game menus

lecture_notes.introduction: Characters do not exist only in the game world. They appear in menus, inventory screens, dialogue boxes, loading screens, achievement notifications, and even error messages. Each UI context has different requirements: a character portrait in a dialogue box needs to be readable at 64x64 pixels. A character on the title screen needs to be impressive at 4K resolution. A character icon in a party list needs to be identifiable in a glance. Consistent character representation across all these contexts reinforces the character's identity and the game's visual cohesion. Some games go further: Persona 5 infuses every menu with the protagonist's rebellious personality through angular shapes, bold colors, and animations that break UI conventions. The character is not just displayed in the UI -- the UI itself is an expression of the character. Today we study how character design extends into UI and system design, creating a consistent character presence that reinforces personality at every touchpoint.
key_concepts: Character portraits: small images representing characters in dialogue, party lists, and status screens. Must be readable at minimum size (32x32 pixels). Design priorities: face visibility, distinctive color, and expression that communicates the character's default emotional state.

Full-body UI renders: character art for menus, equipment screens, and marketing. These are often higher quality than in-game renders, showing detail that gameplay camera distance obscures. Must match the in-game model's proportions and colors.

Character-driven UI: menus and interfaces that express the character's personality through design language. Persona 5's sharp angles and red/black palette reflect Joker's rebellious spirit. Animal Crossing's rounded, pastel UI reflects the cozy aesthetic.

Loading screen characters: characters posed, animated, or accompanied by dialogue during loading screens maintain engagement and reinforce personality. Tips attributed to specific characters ('Elara says: remember to check your inventory!') are more engaging than generic tips.

Dialogue box design: the visual framing of character dialogue. Portrait position (left for protagonist, right for NPC), name plate styling, text box shape, and background treatment all communicate character status and relationship.

Character in HUD elements: health bars styled to match character aesthetic (organic vines for a druid, digital readouts for a cyborg), ability icons that match the character's visual language, and ultimate/special indicators that reflect the character's signature move.

Emotion icons and emotes: simplified character expressions for communication systems (multiplayer emotes, reaction icons, status indicators). Must be readable at very small sizes and culturally understandable.

Marketing material consistency: the character's appearance in trailers, box art, social media, and merchandise must match their in-game representation. Visual inconsistency between marketing and gameplay breaks player trust.

Accessibility in character UI: ensuring character UI elements work for players with visual impairments. High contrast portraits, text alternatives for character status indicators, and screen reader compatible character descriptions.

Character UI animation: subtle animations in menu character renders (breathing, blinking, weight shifting, idle personality animations) bring menus to life. Over-animated UI is distracting; under-animated UI feels static.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a character's full UI presence: creating a 64x64 portrait that is readable and expressive, a dialogue box layout with character-appropriate styling, a health bar design that reflects the character's visual identity, and a menu screen composition that uses the character as the visual anchor.
setup_requirements: Digital art application for portrait and UI mockup creation
UI wireframing tool (Figma, Sketch, or Adobe XD)
Reference screenshots of character UI from published games
Minimum size readability testing at 32x32, 64x64, and 128x128 pixels

review.value: A character portrait must be identifiable at 32x32 pixels. This means: face centered, distinctive color, minimal background detail, and expression that communicates the character's default state. Testing at minimum size reveals whether the portrait design works.

The UI itself can express character personality. Sharp angles and dark colors for a rebellious character (Persona 5). Organic curves and warm tones for a nature character. The character's design language extends beyond their body into every screen they touch.

The character in the trailer must look like the character in the game. Inconsistency (a detailed cinematic model in marketing, a simplified model in gameplay) breaks player trust. The model sheet ensures all representations share the same proportions, colors, and personality.

Health bars, ability icons, and status indicators styled to match the character's visual identity reinforce the player's connection to their avatar. A druid's health bar made of intertwining vines feels different from a soldier's military status display, even if they function identically.

learning_objectives: Design characters that draw from diverse cultural traditions with respect, accuracy, and intentionality
Identify and avoid harmful stereotypes, cultural appropriation, and reductive cultural representations in character design
Apply consultation practices to ensure cultural representations are authentic and respectful
Understand the business and ethical imperatives for inclusive character design in a global market
readings: GDC 2021: 'Cultural Sensitivity in Character Design' (Ta-Nehisi Coates panel)
Designing Games for Ethics (Miguel Sicart) Ch. 5-7
Game Accessibility and Inclusion Guidelines (IGDA)

lecture_notes.introduction: Games are a global medium played by people from every culture on earth. Character designers have both an opportunity and a responsibility: an opportunity to introduce players to cultures they have never encountered, and a responsibility to represent those cultures with accuracy and respect rather than reducing them to costumes and stereotypes. Cultural sensitivity is not about avoiding non-Western influences -- it is about engaging with them deeply enough to represent them authentically. A Japanese game designer creating a character inspired by West African mythology should research that mythology, consult with people from that culture, and design a character that members of that culture would recognize as respectful rather than reductive. Today we study cultural sensitivity as a practical design skill: how to research, consult, and design characters that are culturally rich without being culturally harmful. This is also a business imperative -- games that misrepresent cultures face backlash, boycotts, and lost revenue in the very markets they were trying to appeal to.
key_concepts: Cultural research depth: surface research (Googling 'Indian mythology') produces stereotypes. Deep research (reading primary sources, studying historical context, understanding cultural significance) produces authentic characters. Budget research time proportional to the cultural content.

Consultation: involving people from the represented culture in the design process. This can range from hiring cultural consultants to running focus groups with community members. Consultation catches blind spots that research alone cannot reveal.

Stereotype identification: recognizing common harmful stereotypes (the 'mystical Asian,' the 'noble savage,' the 'hot-tempered Latina,' the 'scary Black man') and designing characters that transcend these reductions. Characters should be individuals first, cultural representatives second.

Cultural appropriation vs appreciation: appropriation takes cultural elements out of context for aesthetic value without understanding or crediting their significance. Appreciation engages with cultural meaning, credits sources, and enriches the representation.

Religious and sacred elements: some cultural elements (sacred symbols, religious ceremonies, ancestral traditions) are not appropriate for entertainment use. Designers must research what is and is not permissible within each cultural context.

Character complexity over tokenism: a single character representing an entire culture is tokenism. A roster with multiple characters from the same culture, each with different personalities and roles, represents that culture as diverse rather than monolithic.

Localization awareness: character names, clothing, and dialogue that work in one cultural context may be offensive in another. Localization teams should review character content for each market before release.

Historical context: designing characters inspired by historical cultures requires understanding the power dynamics of that history. A character design that would be read as 'cool warrior' in one culture might be read as 'glorification of oppression' in another.

Positive representation impact: players who see their culture respectfully represented report increased connection, satisfaction, and loyalty to the game. Diverse representation expands the audience and builds brand trust.
Iterative cultural review: cultural sensitivity is not a checkbox but an ongoing process. Design reviews should include cultural evaluation at concept, production, and pre-release stages.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study analysis: reviewing three published game characters (one well-done cultural representation, one problematic one, and one that improved through community feedback and patches). Identifying what made the good example work, what made the problematic example harmful, and how the improved example was fixed. Then applying the lessons to evaluate a hypothetical character concept.
setup_requirements: Case study materials with analyzed characters (provided in course materials)
Cultural sensitivity evaluation checklist
Discussion guidelines for respectful classroom conversation
Guest speaker or video from a cultural consultant (if available)

review.value: Surface research produces stereotypes. Deep research (primary sources, historical context, cultural significance) produces authentic characters. A character inspired by Yoruba mythology should reflect someone who has read Yoruba stories, not someone who searched 'African mythology' on Pinterest.

Involving members of the represented culture in the design process catches blind spots that no amount of research can reveal. This can be formal (hired cultural consultant) or informal (community focus group). The key is listening and being willing to change the design based on feedback.

One character representing an entire culture is tokenism. Multiple characters from the same culture with different personalities, roles, and visual designs represents that culture as diverse and complex. Diversity within representation is as important as diversity of representation.

Games are global products. Misrepresenting a culture alienates that market. Respectful representation builds loyalty and expands the audience. The business case and the ethical case align: doing right by cultures is also doing right by the business.

learning_objectives: Compile semester character work into a professional portfolio suitable for industry applications
Present character design processes with clear visual documentation including concept evolution, model sheets, and animation samples
Write portfolio case studies that demonstrate design thinking from concept through production-ready deliverables
Understand industry expectations for junior character designer and character artist portfolios
readings: Character Design Portfolio Best Practices (ArtStation blog)
GDC 2020: 'Getting a Character Design Job' (Raf Grassetti)
Industry portfolio review feedback compilation (provided in course materials)

lecture_notes.introduction: A character design portfolio is evidence that you can create compelling, production-ready game characters through a disciplined design process. Industry reviewers spend 60-90 seconds on initial portfolio evaluation, looking for three things: (1) strong visual design fundamentals (shape language, color, silhouette readability), (2) production-ready deliverables (model sheets that a 3D artist could use), and (3) design thinking (the ability to articulate why you made each choice). Today we compile all semester work into portfolio-ready case studies, focusing on presenting the design process rather than just the final result. Studios hire designers who can solve character design problems through research, iteration, and reasoned decision-making. A portfolio that shows only beautiful final illustrations misses the design thinking that hiring managers value most. The strongest portfolios include concept exploration, design rationale, process documentation, and honest assessment of what worked and what could be improved.
key_concepts: Portfolio structure: 3-5 character projects, each as a complete case study. Quality over quantity. One exceptional character with full process documentation beats five pretty illustrations with no context.

Case study format: (1) character brief (role, genre, target audience), (2) concept exploration (10-15 thumbnail sketches showing variation), (3) design decisions with rationale, (4) final model sheet with turnarounds and expression sheet, (5) personality profile and bark script (if applicable), (6) reflection on what works and what could improve.

Process documentation: showing the evolution from early thumbnails through refined concepts to final model sheets. This evidence of iteration demonstrates design skill because it shows the designer can evaluate and improve their own work.

Presentation quality: consistent formatting, readable layout, appropriate image resolution, and professional written communication. The portfolio's visual quality signals the designer's attention to detail and aesthetic sensibility.

Diverse project range: showing characters for different genres (fantasy RPG, sci-fi shooter, cozy sim), different roles (protagonist, antagonist, NPC, companion), and different visual styles (realistic, stylized, pixel art) demonstrates versatility.

Design rationale writing: for each major design decision, explain the 'why' in 1-2 sentences. 'I chose triangular shapes for the villain because triangles communicate danger and aggression, reinforcing her threatening presence before the player encounters her dialogue.'
Digital presence: hosting the portfolio on a personal website with easy navigation, fast loading, and mobile compatibility. Include links to animation reels and 3D model viewers when applicable.

Tailoring for roles: character designer portfolios emphasize visual design and personality. Character artist portfolios emphasize 3D modeling and texturing. Technical artist portfolios emphasize rigging and procedural generation. Know which role you are targeting.
Feedback integration: before submitting the portfolio, get feedback from peers and mentors. Fresh eyes catch layout issues, unclear rationale, and weak projects that should be removed.
Continuous improvement: the portfolio is a living document. Update it with new projects, remove weaker pieces as stronger ones are added, and refine presentation based on feedback from applications.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Reviewing two example character design portfolios (one strong, one weak) and identifying what makes the strong one effective.
