Then live-editing a student's case study: selecting the best concept explorations, writing concise design rationale, and structuring the presentation for maximum impact in a 90-second review.
setup_requirements: All semester deliverables compiled digitally
Portfolio template (web or PDF, provided in course materials)
Peer review rubric for portfolio feedback
Screen capture tool for presentation materials

review.value: Studios hire designers who can think through character design problems, not just illustrators who can draw pretty pictures. Showing concept exploration, design rationale, and iteration evidence demonstrates the design thinking that hiring managers value most.

Reviewers spend 60-90 seconds on initial evaluation. The first page must communicate: this person can design compelling game characters with strong fundamentals. Lead with your best project, not your biography.

Every major design choice should be explained in 1-2 sentences. 'I chose round shapes for the companion because circles communicate approachability, and players need to trust this character immediately.' Rationale demonstrates intentionality.

Show characters for different genres, roles, and visual styles. A portfolio with five fantasy warriors demonstrates one skill set. A portfolio with a fantasy warrior, a sci-fi pilot, a cozy sim NPC, a horror antagonist, and a stylized mascot demonstrates versatility.

learning_objectives: Present a complete character design package integrating all course concepts from visual design through behavioral systems
Conduct structured peer reviews providing actionable feedback on character design portfolios
Reflect on personal design growth over the semester through honest post-mortem analysis
Evaluate character designs against professional quality standards for industry readiness
readings: Course review materials covering all 14 previous sessions
Peer review rubric and presentation guidelines (provided in course materials)

lecture_notes.introduction: This is the culmination of the semester. Over 14 weeks you have studied and practiced every facet of game character design: archetypes and personality, visual design language, shape and silhouette theory, model sheets, character writing, animation principles, facial expressions, motion capture, NPC behavior, companion AI, boss design, customization systems, UI representation, and cultural sensitivity. Today you present your final character: a complete design package that integrates visual design, personality profile, animation considerations, and behavioral design into a cohesive character that is ready for production. The presentation is not just a gallery showing -- it is a design defense. You will explain your archetype choices, justify your shape language, demonstrate how your character's personality drives their behavior, and honestly assess what works and what needs improvement. Your peers will provide structured feedback, and you will review two of their characters in return. The standard is not perfection but demonstrable design thinking: a character with known weaknesses and a thoughtful plan for addressing them is more impressive than a character the designer claims is flawless.
key_concepts: Presentation structure: (1) character brief and context (1 minute), (2) visual design walkthrough with shape language and color rationale (3 minutes), (3) model sheet and expression sheet review (2 minutes), (4) personality profile and motivation triad (2 minutes), (5) behavioral design or animation considerations (2 minutes), (6) post-mortem: what works, what does not, what you would change (2 minutes).

Design defense: explaining not just what you designed but why. 'I chose triangular shapes because this character is the primary antagonist and triangles communicate danger' is a design argument. 'I chose triangles because they look cool' is not.

Integration evaluation: does the visual design match the personality? Does the animation style match the character's weight and energy? Do the barks sound like the character voice? Integration coherence is the hallmark of professional character design.
Peer review: using the course rubric to evaluate shape language, color design, personality depth, production readiness, and design rationale. Feedback must be specific and actionable.
Post-mortem honesty: identifying weaknesses in your own work demonstrates self-awareness and growth potential. Studios value designers who can critically evaluate their own output.
Growth documentation: comparing your first character sketches from week 1 with your final portfolio-ready character. The gap is the tangible measure of semester learning.
Professional readiness assessment: evaluating whether each student's portfolio meets the minimum standard for a junior character designer application. Identifying specific improvements needed.

Career paths in character design: concept artist, character modeler, character technical artist (rigging), character animator, character designer (narrative-focused), character art director. Understanding specializations helps focus career development.
Continued learning: resources for ongoing improvement including online communities (ArtStation, Character Design Challenge), books, workshops, and game jam participation.

Industry networking: how to present character work at industry events, portfolio reviews, and online communities. Building relationships with working professionals through quality work and genuine engagement.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Student final presentations: 12-minute slots per student including character walkthrough, design defense, and post-mortem. Peer review forms completed during each presentation. Instructor feedback on design quality, portfolio readiness, and growth areas.
setup_requirements: Completed final character design package
Portfolio-ready presentation materials
Peer review rubric sheets
Screen sharing and projection for presentations

review.value: Every visual and narrative choice should be justified by design principles, character role, or target audience. The ability to articulate why you made each decision separates designers from artists. 'Because it communicates X to the player' is the strongest form of design argument.

The ultimate test of a character design: does every element (visual, personality, animation, behavior, UI) tell the same story about who this character is? Inconsistencies (friendly visuals + aggressive behavior) break the character's credibility.

The final character does not need to be flawless. It needs to demonstrate design thinking, visual fundamentals, and the ability to create production-ready deliverables. A flawed character with thoughtful analysis beats a polished character with no process.

Admitting that the shape language is inconsistent, the personality profile needs more depth, or the expression sheet lacks range demonstrates professional self-awareness. This honesty is more valuable to hiring managers than false confidence.

quizzes.topic: Character Archetypes, Visual Design, and Model Sheets

questions.question: You are designing a villain character for a fantasy RPG. The character is a cunning, manipulative sorcerer who avoids direct confrontation. Describe the shape language, color palette, and silhouette characteristics you would use, and explain how each choice communicates the character's personality to the player before any dialogue occurs.
questions.answer: Shape language: predominantly triangular -- sharp shoulders, pointed collar, angular facial features, tapered fingers. Triangles communicate danger and aggression, and when combined with elongated proportions, they suggest intelligence and precision rather than brute force. Color palette: deep purples and blacks as primary colors (mystery, power, corruption) with acid green or gold accents (toxicity or greed). Low saturation on the body with high saturation on focal points (glowing eyes, enchanted jewelry) draws attention to the sources of magical power. Silhouette: tall and narrow with a distinctive asymmetric feature (one oversized sleeve, a staff, or a flowing cape that breaks the body outline). The silhouette should be instantly distinguishable from the hero's broader, more grounded shape at any distance.

rubric.key: Appropriate shape language with reasoning tied to personality
Color palette choices with psychological justification
Silhouette design that is distinctive and readable

questions.question: Explain why a character sheet requires turnaround views drawn on a proportion grid at consistent scale, rather than just a single front-facing illustration. Identify at least three production roles that use the character sheet and one specific problem it prevents.
questions.answer: A single front-facing illustration leaves the 3D modeler guessing about the character's profile depth, back design, and side proportions. The proportion grid ensures all views are drawn at identical scale so measurements match exactly. Production roles that use the sheet: (1) the 3D modeler builds the base mesh matching proportions from every angle, (2) the texture artist paints consistent details across UV maps using the callout panels, (3) the rigger places joints based on visible joint positions in the side view. The sheet prevents asymmetric models where the front looks correct but the side has wrong proportions because the modeler guessed the depth.

rubric.key: Explains the limitation of a single view for 3D production
Identifies at least 3 production roles using the sheet
Describes a specific problem the consistent-scale turnaround prevents

questions.question: Define the motivation triad (want, need, fear) and create a triad for an RPG companion character who is a former thief trying to redeem themselves. Explain how the tension between want and need creates depth.
questions.answer: The motivation triad: Want is the conscious goal the character pursues. Need is the unconscious flaw they must overcome. Fear is the consequence of failure. For the former thief companion: Want: prove they have changed by helping the hero complete their quest (conscious, outward goal). Need: forgive themselves for the people they hurt as a thief, rather than seeking others' approval as a substitute for self-forgiveness (unconscious, inward need). Fear: being rejected by the hero after revealing their past, confirming they can never escape their former identity. The tension: pursuing approval (want) without addressing self-forgiveness (need) means the character becomes a people-pleaser who suppresses their true feelings. This creates dramatic moments where the mask slips -- they react harshly when accused of dishonesty because the accusation touches their unforgiven guilt.

rubric.key: Correctly defines all three elements of the motivation triad
Creates a specific, coherent triad for the companion character
Explains how want/need tension creates character depth and drama

questions.question: Explain how the animation principle of anticipation serves a dual purpose in game design: both as an aesthetic animation principle and as a gameplay communication tool. Give a specific example of an enemy attack where removing the anticipation frames would make the game feel unfair.
questions.answer: Aesthetically, anticipation makes movement feel physically grounded and satisfying -- a character crouches before jumping, pulls back before swinging. Without it, actions feel weightless and robotic. As a gameplay tool, anticipation frames are attack telegraphs that give the player time to read and react. For example, in Dark Souls, a large enemy's overhead slam has 15-20 frames of wind-up (raising the weapon high, shifting weight back) before the fast downswing. These frames are the player's entire reaction window for dodging. Removing the wind-up means the attack goes from idle to impact in 2-3 frames, giving zero reaction time. The attack becomes unavoidable regardless of the player's skill, making the game feel unfair. Anticipation frame duration directly controls difficulty.

rubric.key: Explains anticipation as aesthetic principle (weight, physicality)
Explains anticipation as gameplay telegraph (reaction window)
Specific example showing why removal makes the game unfair

questions.question: Compare character voice in writing with visual shape language. How does each communicate personality, and why must they be consistent? Give an example of a character where voice and shape language conflict.
questions.answer: Shape language communicates personality through visual geometry: round shapes say friendly, triangles say dangerous, squares say stable. Character voice communicates personality through speech patterns: vocabulary, sentence length, tone, and verbal tics. Both must be consistent because they deliver the same message through different channels. A character with round, soft visual design who speaks in short, aggressive, militaristic sentences creates cognitive dissonance -- the player receives conflicting signals about who this character is. Example: a character designed with Kirby-like round proportions and pastel colors (visually friendly) who speaks in cold, calculating sentences with formal vocabulary ('Your compliance is required. Resistance is inadvisable.') would confuse the player about whether to trust them. This conflict could be intentional (a deceptively friendly villain) but must be a deliberate design choice, not an accident.

rubric.key: Explains how shape language communicates personality
Explains how character voice communicates personality
Gives a concrete example of voice/shape conflict with consequences

questions.question: What is the Duchenne smile and why is it critical for avoiding the uncanny valley in facial animation? Describe the specific FACS Action Units involved.
questions.answer: A Duchenne smile is a genuine smile that involves both the mouth and the eyes. It requires AU12 (lip corner pull -- the mouth curving upward) combined with AU6 (cheek raise -- the orbicularis oculi muscle around the eyes contracting, creating 'crow's feet' and pushing up the lower eyelids). A non-Duchenne smile uses only AU12 without AU6, producing a 'polite smile' that does not reach the eyes. In facial animation, characters whose smiles lack AU6 trigger uncanny valley discomfort because humans are subconsciously expert at detecting fake smiles. We evolved this ability to detect deception in social interactions. A character model that only animates the mouth during a smile looks insincere, untrustworthy, or creepy rather than happy. Adding AU6 to smile animations is the single most impactful improvement for avoiding uncanny-feeling facial animation.

rubric.key: Correctly defines the Duchenne smile and contrasts with non-Duchenne
Identifies AU6 and AU12 as the specific FACS Action Units
Explains the connection to uncanny valley in animation

quizzes.topic: Motion Capture, NPC Design, and Companion AI

questions.question: Describe three specific design rules for companion AI that prevent the companion from becoming a liability. For each rule, name a game that violates it and explain the resulting negative player experience.
questions.answer: Rule 1: Never block the player's movement path. The companion must yield doorways and corridors. Violation: In early Resident Evil 5, Sheva would stand in doorways during combat, trapping the player. Players felt frustrated because death was caused by their ally. Rule 2: Never steal kills or glory moments. The companion should assist but let the player land finishing blows. Violation: In some Skyrim companion configurations, followers rush ahead and kill enemies before engagement, making the player feel like a spectator. Rule 3: Never require the player to micromanage the companion's survival. Violation: In many escort missions (early Halo, Dead Rising), the escorted NPC walks into enemy fire and dies, forcing mission failure for something outside the player's control. This creates helpless frustration.

rubric.key: Three distinct, specific design rules stated clearly
Valid game examples that violate each rule
Clear explanation of the negative player experience

questions.question: Explain NPC behavior layers (ambient, reactive, interactive) and describe how a blacksmith NPC would behave at each layer. How do these layers create the illusion of a living character?
questions.answer: Ambient layer (default behavior): The blacksmith hammers at the anvil, inspects finished items, wipes sweat from their brow, stokes the forge fire, and occasionally takes a drink from a water flask. These activities run on a randomized loop during work hours. Reactive layer (responds to world events): If it rains, the blacksmith moves under cover and examines indoor inventory. If the player draws a weapon nearby, the blacksmith backs away and says 'easy there.' If a fire breaks out, the blacksmith grabs a bucket. Interactive layer (responds to player): When the player approaches, the blacksmith stops hammering, makes eye contact, and greets them. If the player has a quest item the blacksmith wants, they comment on it. During trading, they examine offered items and comment on quality. Together, these layers create the illusion of a character with awareness, routine, and personality because the NPC appears to have a life independent of the player (ambient), awareness of the world (reactive), and social presence (interactive).

rubric.key: Correctly describes all three behavior layers with definitions
Provides specific, varied examples for the blacksmith at each layer
Explains how the layers together create the illusion of a living character

questions.question: When should a game studio use motion capture vs hand-keyed animation? Describe one scenario where mo-cap is clearly superior and one where keyframe is clearly superior, with reasoning.
questions.answer: Mo-cap is clearly superior for: a realistic AAA narrative game with extensive dialogue scenes (like The Last of Us). Performance capture records body, face, and voice simultaneously, producing integrated performances where subtle facial expressions naturally match dialogue inflections and body language. Hand-animating this level of nuanced, naturalistic acting for hundreds of dialogue scenes would be prohibitively expensive and likely less convincing. Keyframe is clearly superior for: a stylized action game with precise combat timing requirements (like Guilty Gear Strive). The exaggerated squash-and-stretch, held poses, and precise frame timing that define the game's visual identity cannot be captured from real human movement. Each attack needs frame-perfect timing for gameplay balance, and the extreme stylization requires manual posing that exceeds physical human capability.

rubric.key: Describes a scenario where mo-cap is clearly superior with reasoning
Describes a scenario where keyframe is clearly superior with reasoning
Reasoning addresses both quality and practical considerations

quizzes.topic: Boss Design, Customization, and Character in UI

questions.question: Design a two-phase boss fight for a fantasy RPG. Describe the boss's personality, how each phase reflects a different aspect of that personality, and what skill each phase tests. Include the visual change that marks the phase transition.
questions.answer: Boss: an arrogant dragon knight who believes they are invincible. Phase 1 (Confident): The knight fights with slow, powerful sword swings with long telegraphs (1-2 second wind-up), taunting the player between attacks ('Is this the best this kingdom sends?'). They leave long recovery windows after combos. This phase tests pattern recognition and dodge timing using skills taught throughout the game. Visual: gleaming golden armor, upright posture, cape flowing. Phase 2 (Desperate): At 40% health, the player breaks the knight's helmet, revealing a scarred face. The knight's personality shifts to fury -- attacks become faster with shorter telegraphs, they stop taunting and start snarling, and they add a new charge attack. Recovery windows are shorter. This tests the player's ability to adapt to changed timing while maintaining the dodge skills from Phase 1. Visual transition: helmet shatters, armor cracks revealing dark underlayer, cape burns away, fighting stance becomes hunched and aggressive.

rubric.key: Boss personality clearly defined and reflected in combat behavior
Two distinct phases with different mechanics and skills tested
Visual transition that communicates the phase change and personality shift

questions.question: In a multiplayer hero shooter, how do you allow extensive character customization while preserving gameplay readability? Describe two specific techniques and explain what problem each solves.
questions.answer: Technique 1: Preserve class-defining silhouettes across all cosmetic options. Every skin for a tank character maintains their wide, bulky silhouette. Every skin for a sniper maintains their tall, thin silhouette. Cosmetics change textures, colors, and surface details but never alter the character's fundamental body proportions. This solves the identification problem: players can identify the enemy's class and expected behavior from the silhouette alone, even if they have never seen that specific skin before. Technique 2: Faction or team coloring on non-customizable elements. Certain UI elements (health bars, nameplates) and character elements (team-colored accents, outline highlights) cannot be customized and always display team affiliation. This solves the friend-or-foe problem: regardless of how wild the player's cosmetic choices are, the team-colored elements always clearly indicate whether they are an ally or enemy.

rubric.key: First technique clearly described with the specific problem it solves
Second technique clearly described with the specific problem it solves
Both techniques address gameplay readability specifically

questions.question: Explain why character portraits for UI must be designed at minimum display size (32x32 or 64x64 pixels) rather than at full illustration resolution and then scaled down. What specific design changes are needed for portraits that work at small sizes?
questions.answer: Scaling down a full-resolution illustration to 64x64 pixels causes fine details to blur into indistinct noise. Hair strands become a muddy blob, subtle facial features disappear, and secondary design elements merge. A portrait designed at minimum display size prioritizes the elements that remain readable at that scale. Design changes needed: (1) Simplify the face to its most distinctive features -- a scar, unique hair shape, or eye color becomes the primary identifier. (2) Increase color contrast between the face and background so the face reads clearly against the UI context. (3) Remove busy backgrounds -- a solid color or simple gradient provides maximum face readability. (4) Enlarge the character's signature feature (distinctive hair, helmet shape, eye marking) so it dominates the portrait area. (5) Use rim lighting or outline to separate the character from the background at any size.

rubric.key: Explains why scaling down fails (detail loss, blur, noise)
Describes at least 3 specific design changes for small-size portraits
Changes address readability at minimum display size

quizzes.topic: Cultural Sensitivity, Portfolio, and Design Integration

questions.question: Explain the difference between cultural appropriation and cultural appreciation in character design. Give one example of each in a published game character, and describe what makes one harmful and the other respectful.
questions.answer: Cultural appropriation takes cultural elements out of context for aesthetic value without understanding their significance. Example: a fighting game character whose 'costume' is a Native American headdress used as generic decoration, ignoring that war bonnets are sacred items earned through specific acts of honor in Plains Indian cultures. This is harmful because it reduces a living culture's sacred practice to a costume accessory. Cultural appreciation engages with cultural meaning, researches context, and enriches the representation. Example: Ghost of Tsushima's depiction of samurai culture involved extensive research, consultation with Japanese historians, and a commitment to portraying Tsushima's history with respect. The game was praised by Japanese audiences and critics for its attention to cultural detail. This is respectful because the cultural elements serve the story's authenticity rather than being decorative props.

rubric.key: Correctly distinguishes appropriation from appreciation
Provides a valid example of appropriation with explanation of harm
Provides a valid example of appreciation with explanation of respect

questions.question: What three things does a hiring manager look for in the first 90 seconds of reviewing a character design portfolio? For each, describe one portfolio element that demonstrates it.
questions.answer: (1) Visual design fundamentals: strong shape language, effective color use, and readable silhouettes. Demonstrated by: a character model sheet with turnaround views where the shape language is consistent across all angles, the silhouette is distinctive, and the color palette is intentional. (2) Production-ready deliverables: model sheets that a 3D artist could build from without additional guidance. Demonstrated by: proportion grid turnarounds with material callouts, exact color hex values, and expression sheets showing the full facial range. (3) Design thinking: the ability to explain why each choice was made. Demonstrated by: a one-page design rationale connecting shape language to personality, color choices to character role, and costume elements to backstory and game context.

rubric.key: Identifies visual fundamentals with specific demonstration element
Identifies production readiness with specific demonstration element
Identifies design thinking with specific demonstration element

questions.question: Describe a design consistency audit for a character. What specific checks would you perform to ensure that visual design, personality writing, animation style, and behavioral design all reinforce the same character identity?
questions.answer: A design consistency audit systematically checks that every aspect of the character communicates the same identity. Checks: (1) Shape-personality alignment: do the visual shapes match the personality? A friendly character (warm personality diamond) should have round, soft shapes, not angular, aggressive ones. (2) Color-role alignment: does the color palette match the character's faction and role? An ally should not use the villain faction's color scheme. (3) Voice-visual alignment: does the written dialogue voice match what the visual design suggests? A character who looks scholarly should not speak in slang unless the contrast is intentional. (4) Animation-weight alignment: does the animation style match the character's visual mass and energy? A heavy character should not have bouncy, lightweight animations. (5) Behavior-personality alignment: do the companion AI barks and behavioral priorities reflect the personality diamond values? An introverted character should not constantly initiate conversations.

rubric.key: Describes at least 4 specific alignment checks
Checks span visual, written, animation, and behavioral domains
Explains what inconsistency would look like for at least one check

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes assessing understanding of character design theory, animation principles, NPC behavior, and cultural sensitivity

lab_assignments.description: Weekly character sketching, modeling, and design exercises building visual communication and production skills

major_assignments.description: Three projects: complete character design package, animated character with companion AI, and boss character with encounter design

midterm_examination.description: Written and practical exam covering visual design fundamentals, personality construction, animation principles, and FACS

final_project.description: Portfolio-quality final character presentation with design defense, peer review, and post-mortem analysis

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all critique sessions and live demonstrations. More than 2 unexcused absences will reduce the participation grade by one full letter per additional absence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Design documents and written deliverables lose 10% per day late. Art deliverables (character sheets, animation reels) lose 15% per day late due to their role in peer critique scheduling. No submissions accepted more than 4 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: All character designs must be original individual work. You may use classmates as figure drawing references but may not base your character design on another student's work. Peer critique is collaborative; design execution is individual.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Reference material and artistic inspiration must be cited. Tracing over existing character art is prohibited. Use of AI image generation tools for final deliverables is not permitted; hand-drawn or hand-modeled work is required. AI tools may be used for brainstorming and reference gathering only, with disclosure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students must have a digital drawing tablet (Wacom, Huion, or iPad with Apple Pencil), Blender 3.6+, and a drawing application (Photoshop, Clip Studio Paint, or Procreate). Lab drawing tablets are available for in-class use but personal hardware is strongly recommended for homework.
